# Hooks reference

> Reference for Claude Code hook events, configuration schema, JSON input/output formats, exit codes, async hooks, prompt hooks, and MCP tool hooks.

<Tip>
  For a quickstart guide with examples, see [Automate workflows with hooks](/en/hooks-guide).
</Tip>

Hooks are user-defined shell commands or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Use this reference to look up event schemas, configuration options, JSON input/output formats, and advanced features like async hooks and MCP tool hooks. If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead.

## Hook lifecycle

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, this arrives on stdin. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop:

1000â†’```
  1001â†’
  1002â†’### Notification
  1003â†’
  1004â†’Runs when Claude Code sends notifications. Matches on notification type: `permission_prompt`, `idle_prompt`, `auth_success`, `elicitation_dialog`. Omit the matcher to run hooks for all notification types.
  1005â†’
  1006â†’Use separate matchers to run different handlers depending on the notification type. This configuration triggers a permission-specific alert script when Claude needs permission approval and a different notification when Claude has been idle:
  1007â†’
  1008â†’```json  theme={null}
  1009â†’{
  1010â†’  "hooks": {
  1011â†’    "Notification": [
  1012â†’      {
  1013â†’        "matcher": "permission_prompt",
  1014â†’        "hooks": [
  1015â†’          {
  1016â†’            "type": "command",
  1017â†’            "command": "/path/to/permission-alert.sh"
  1018â†’          }
  1019â†’        ]
  1020â†’      },
  1021â†’      {
  1022â†’        "matcher": "idle_prompt",
  1023â†’        "hooks": [
  1024â†’          {
  1025â†’            "type": "command",
  1026â†’            "command": "/path/to/idle-notification.sh"
  1027â†’          }
  1028â†’        ]
  1029â†’      }
  1030â†’    ]
  1031â†’  }
  1032â†’}
  1033â†’```
  1034â†’
  1035â†’#### Notification input
  1036â†’
  1037â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), Notification hooks receive `message` with the notification text, an optional `title`, and `notification_type` indicating which type fired.
  1038â†’
  1039â†’```json  theme={null}
  1040â†’{
  1041â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1042â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1043â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1044â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1045â†’  "hook_event_name": "Notification",
  1046â†’  "message": "Claude needs your permission to use Bash",
  1047â†’  "title": "Permission needed",
  1048â†’  "notification_type": "permission_prompt"
  1049â†’}
  1050â†’```
  1051â†’
  1052â†’Notification hooks cannot block or modify notifications. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, you can return `additionalContext` to add context to the conversation:
  1053â†’
  1054â†’| Field               | Description                      |
  1055â†’| :------------------ | :------------------------------- |
  1056â†’| `additionalContext` | String added to Claude's context |
  1057â†’
  1058â†’### SubagentStart
  1059â†’
  1060â†’Runs when a Claude Code subagent is spawned via the Task tool. Supports matchers to filter by agent type name (built-in agents like `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names from `.claude/agents/`).
  1061â†’
  1062â†’#### SubagentStart input
  1063â†’
  1064â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SubagentStart hooks receive `agent_id` with the unique identifier for the subagent and `agent_type` with the agent name (built-in agents like `"Bash"`, `"Explore"`, `"Plan"`, or custom agent names).
  1065â†’
  1066â†’```json  theme={null}
  1067â†’{
  1068â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1069â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1070â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1071â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1072â†’  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStart",
  1073â†’  "agent_id": "agent-abc123",
  1074â†’  "agent_type": "Explore"
  1075â†’}
  1076â†’```
  1077â†’
  1078â†’SubagentStart hooks cannot block subagent creation, but they can inject context into the subagent. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, you can return:
  1079â†’
  1080â†’| Field               | Description                            |
  1081â†’| :------------------ | :------------------------------------- |
  1082â†’| `additionalContext` | String added to the subagent's context |
  1083â†’
  1084â†’```json  theme={null}
  1085â†’{
  1086â†’  "hookSpecificOutput": {
  1087â†’    "hookEventName": "SubagentStart",
  1088â†’    "additionalContext": "Follow security guidelines for this task"
  1089â†’  }
  1090â†’}
  1091â†’```
  1092â†’
  1093â†’### SubagentStop
  1094â†’
  1095â†’Runs when a Claude Code subagent has finished responding. Matches on agent type, same values as SubagentStart.
  1096â†’
  1097â†’#### SubagentStop input
  1098â†’
  1099â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SubagentStop hooks receive `stop_hook_active`, `agent_id`, `agent_type`, `agent_transcript_path`, and `last_assistant_message`. The `agent_type` field is the value used for matcher filtering. The `transcript_path` is the main session's transcript, while `agent_transcript_path` is the subagent's own transcript stored in a nested `subagents/` folder. The `last_assistant_message` field contains the text content of the subagent's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file.
  1100â†’
  1101â†’```json  theme={null}
  1102â†’{
  1103â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1104â†’  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123.jsonl",
  1105â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1106â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1107â†’  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStop",
  1108â†’  "stop_hook_active": false,
  1109â†’  "agent_id": "def456",
  1110â†’  "agent_type": "Explore",
  1111â†’  "agent_transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123/subagents/agent-def456.jsonl",
  1112â†’  "last_assistant_message": "Analysis complete. Found 3 potential issues..."
  1113â†’}
  1114â†’```
  1115â†’
  1116â†’SubagentStop hooks use the same decision control format as [Stop hooks](#stop-decision-control).
  1117â†’
  1118â†’### Stop
  1119â†’
  1120â†’Runs when the main Claude Code agent has finished responding. Does not run if
  1121â†’the stoppage occurred due to a user interrupt.
  1122â†’
  1123â†’#### Stop input
  1124â†’
  1125â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), Stop hooks receive `stop_hook_active` and `last_assistant_message`. The `stop_hook_active` field is `true` when Claude Code is already continuing as a result of a stop hook. Check this value or process the transcript to prevent Claude Code from running indefinitely. The `last_assistant_message` field contains the text content of Claude's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file.
  1126â†’
  1127â†’```json  theme={null}
  1128â†’{
  1129â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1130â†’  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1131â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1132â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1133â†’  "hook_event_name": "Stop",
  1134â†’  "stop_hook_active": true,
  1135â†’  "last_assistant_message": "I've completed the refactoring. Here's a summary..."
  1136â†’}
  1137â†’```
  1138â†’
  1139â†’#### Stop decision control
  1140â†’
  1141â†’`Stop` and `SubagentStop` hooks can control whether Claude continues. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, your hook script can return these event-specific fields:
  1142â†’
  1143â†’| Field      | Description                                                                |
  1144â†’| :--------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  1145â†’| `decision` | `"block"` prevents Claude from stopping. Omit to allow Claude to stop      |
  1146â†’| `reason`   | Required when `decision` is `"block"`. Tells Claude why it should continue |
  1147â†’
  1148â†’```json  theme={null}
  1149â†’{
  1150â†’  "decision": "block",
  1151â†’  "reason": "Must be provided when Claude is blocked from stopping"
  1152â†’}
  1153â†’```
  1154â†’
  1155â†’### TeammateIdle
  1156â†’
  1157â†’Runs when an [agent team](/en/agent-teams) teammate is about to go idle after finishing its turn. Use this to enforce quality gates before a teammate stops working, such as requiring passing lint checks or verifying that output files exist.
  1158â†’
  1159â†’When a `TeammateIdle` hook exits with code 2, the teammate receives the stderr message as feedback and continues working instead of going idle. TeammateIdle hooks do not support matchers and fire on every occurrence.
  1160â†’
  1161â†’#### TeammateIdle input
  1162â†’
  1163â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), TeammateIdle hooks receive `teammate_name` and `team_name`.
  1164â†’
  1165â†’```json  theme={null}
  1166â†’{
  1167â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1168â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1169â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1170â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1171â†’  "hook_event_name": "TeammateIdle",
  1172â†’  "teammate_name": "researcher",
  1173â†’  "team_name": "my-project"
  1174â†’}
  1175â†’```
  1176â†’
  1177â†’| Field           | Description                                   |
  1178â†’| :-------------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
  1179â†’| `teammate_name` | Name of the teammate that is about to go idle |
  1180â†’| `team_name`     | Name of the team                              |
  1181â†’
  1182â†’#### TeammateIdle decision control
  1183â†’
  1184â†’TeammateIdle hooks use exit codes only, not JSON decision control. This example checks that a build artifact exists before allowing a teammate to go idle:
  1185â†’
  1186â†’```bash  theme={null}
  1187â†’#!/bin/bash
  1188â†’
  1189â†’if [ ! -f "./dist/output.js" ]; then
  1190â†’  echo "Build artifact missing. Run the build before stopping." >&2
  1191â†’  exit 2
  1192â†’fi
  1193â†’
  1194â†’exit 0
  1195â†’```
  1196â†’
  1197â†’### TaskCompleted
  1198â†’
  1199â†’Runs when a task is being marked as completed. This fires in two situations: when any agent explicitly marks a task as completed through the TaskUpdate tool, or when an [agent team](/en/agent-teams) teammate finishes its turn with in-progress tasks. Use this to enforce completion criteria like passing tests or lint checks before a task can close.
  1200â†’
  1201â†’When a `TaskCompleted` hook exits with code 2, the task is not marked as completed and the stderr message is fed back to the model as feedback. TaskCompleted hooks do not support matchers and fire on every occurrence.
  1202â†’
  1203â†’#### TaskCompleted input
  1204â†’
  1205â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), TaskCompleted hooks receive `task_id`, `task_subject`, and optionally `task_description`, `teammate_name`, and `team_name`.
  1206â†’
  1207â†’```json  theme={null}
  1208â†’{
  1209â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1210â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1211â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1212â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1213â†’  "hook_event_name": "TaskCompleted",
  1214â†’  "task_id": "task-001",
  1215â†’  "task_subject": "Implement user authentication",
  1216â†’  "task_description": "Add login and signup endpoints",
  1217â†’  "teammate_name": "implementer",
  1218â†’  "team_name": "my-project"
  1219â†’}
  1220â†’```
  1221â†’
  1222â†’| Field              | Description                                             |
  1223â†’| :----------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
  1224â†’| `task_id`          | Identifier of the task being completed                  |
  1225â†’| `task_subject`     | Title of the task                                       |
  1226â†’| `task_description` | Detailed description of the task. May be absent         |
  1227â†’| `teammate_name`    | Name of the teammate completing the task. May be absent |
  1228â†’| `team_name`        | Name of the team. May be absent                         |
  1229â†’
  1230â†’#### TaskCompleted decision control
  1231â†’
  1232â†’TaskCompleted hooks use exit codes only, not JSON decision control. This example runs tests and blocks task completion if they fail:
  1233â†’
  1234â†’```bash  theme={null}
  1235â†’#!/bin/bash
  1236â†’INPUT=$(cat)
  1237â†’TASK_SUBJECT=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.task_subject')
  1238â†’
  1239â†’# Run the test suite
  1240â†’if ! npm test 2>&1; then
  1241â†’  echo "Tests not passing. Fix failing tests before completing: $TASK_SUBJECT" >&2
  1242â†’  exit 2
  1243â†’fi
  1244â†’
  1245â†’exit 0
  1246â†’```
  1247â†’
  1248â†’### ConfigChange
  1249â†’
  1250â†’Runs when a configuration file changes during a session. Use this to audit settings changes, enforce security policies, or block unauthorized modifications to configuration files.
  1251â†’
  1252â†’ConfigChange hooks fire for changes to settings files, managed policy settings, and skill files. The `source` field in the input tells you which type of configuration changed, and the optional `file_path` field provides the path to the changed file.
  1253â†’
  1254â†’The matcher filters on the configuration source:
  1255â†’
  1256â†’| Matcher            | When it fires                             |
  1257â†’| :----------------- | :---------------------------------------- |
  1258â†’| `user_settings`    | `~/.claude/settings.json` changes         |
  1259â†’| `project_settings` | `.claude/settings.json` changes           |
  1260â†’| `local_settings`   | `.claude/settings.local.json` changes     |
  1261â†’| `policy_settings`  | Managed policy settings change            |
  1262â†’| `skills`           | A skill file in `.claude/skills/` changes |
  1263â†’
  1264â†’This example logs all configuration changes for security auditing:
  1265â†’
  1266â†’```json  theme={null}
  1267â†’{
  1268â†’  "hooks": {
  1269â†’    "ConfigChange": [
  1270â†’      {
  1271â†’        "hooks": [
  1272â†’          {
  1273â†’            "type": "command",
  1274â†’            "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/audit-config-change.sh"
  1275â†’          }
  1276â†’        ]
  1277â†’      }
  1278â†’    ]
  1279â†’  }
  1280â†’}
  1281â†’```
  1282â†’
  1283â†’#### ConfigChange input
  1284â†’
  1285â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), ConfigChange hooks receive `source` and optionally `file_path`. The `source` field indicates which configuration type changed, and `file_path` provides the path to the specific file that was modified.
  1286â†’
  1287â†’```json  theme={null}
  1288â†’{
  1289â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1290â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1291â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1292â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1293â†’  "hook_event_name": "ConfigChange",
  1294â†’  "source": "project_settings",
  1295â†’  "file_path": "/Users/.../my-project/.claude/settings.json"
  1296â†’}
  1297â†’```
  1298â†’
  1299â†’#### ConfigChange decision control
  1300â†’
  1301â†’ConfigChange hooks can block configuration changes from taking effect. Use exit code 2 or a JSON `decision` to prevent the change. When blocked, the new settings are not applied to the running session.
  1302â†’
  1303â†’| Field      | Description                                                                              |
  1304â†’| :--------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  1305â†’| `decision` | `"block"` prevents the configuration change from being applied. Omit to allow the change |
  1306â†’| `reason`   | Explanation shown to the user when `decision` is `"block"`                               |
  1307â†’
  1308â†’```json  theme={null}
  1309â†’{
  1310â†’  "decision": "block",
  1311â†’  "reason": "Configuration changes to project settings require admin approval"
  1312â†’}
  1313â†’```
  1314â†’
  1315â†’`policy_settings` changes cannot be blocked. Hooks still fire for `policy_settings` sources, so you can use them for audit logging, but any blocking decision is ignored. This ensures enterprise-managed settings always take effect.
  1316â†’
  1317â†’### WorktreeCreate
  1318â†’
  1319â†’When you run `claude --worktree` or a [subagent uses `isolation: "worktree"`](/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope), Claude Code creates an isolated working copy using `git worktree`. If you configure a WorktreeCreate hook, it replaces the default git behavior, letting you use a different version control system like SVN, Perforce, or Mercurial.
  1320â†’
  1321â†’The hook must print the absolute path to the created worktree directory on stdout. Claude Code uses this path as the working directory for the isolated session.
  1322â†’
  1323â†’This example creates an SVN working copy and prints the path for Claude Code to use. Replace the repository URL with your own:
  1324â†’
  1325â†’```json  theme={null}
  1326â†’{
  1327â†’  "hooks": {
  1328â†’    "WorktreeCreate": [
  1329â†’      {
  1330â†’        "hooks": [
  1331â†’          {
  1332â†’            "type": "command",
  1333â†’            "command": "bash -c 'NAME=$(jq -r .name); DIR=\"$HOME/.claude/worktrees/$NAME\"; svn checkout https://svn.example.com/repo/trunk \"$DIR\" >&2 && echo \"$DIR\"'"
  1334â†’          }
  1335â†’        ]
  1336â†’      }
  1337â†’    ]
  1338â†’  }
  1339â†’}
  1340â†’```
  1341â†’
  1342â†’The hook reads the worktree `name` from the JSON input on stdin, checks out a fresh copy into a new directory, and prints the directory path. The `echo` on the last line is what Claude Code reads as the worktree path. Redirect any other output to stderr so it doesn't interfere with the path.
  1343â†’
  1344â†’#### WorktreeCreate input
  1345â†’
  1346â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), WorktreeCreate hooks receive the `name` field. This is a slug identifier for the new worktree, either specified by the user or auto-generated (for example, `bold-oak-a3f2`).
  1347â†’
  1348â†’```json  theme={null}
  1349â†’{
  1350â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1351â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1352â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1353â†’  "hook_event_name": "WorktreeCreate",
  1354â†’  "name": "feature-auth"
  1355â†’}
  1356â†’```
  1357â†’
  1358â†’#### WorktreeCreate output
  1359â†’
  1360â†’The hook must print the absolute path to the created worktree directory on stdout. If the hook fails or produces no output, worktree creation fails with an error.
  1361â†’
  1362â†’WorktreeCreate hooks do not use the standard allow/block decision model. Instead, the hook's success or failure determines the outcome. Only `type: "command"` hooks are supported.
  1363â†’
  1364â†’### WorktreeRemove
  1365â†’
  1366â†’The cleanup counterpart to [WorktreeCreate](#worktreecreate). This hook fires when a worktree is being removed, either when you exit a `--worktree` session and choose to remove it, or when a subagent with `isolation: "worktree"` finishes. For git-based worktrees, Claude handles cleanup automatically with `git worktree remove`. If you configured a WorktreeCreate hook for a non-git version control system, pair it with a WorktreeRemove hook to handle cleanup. Without one, the worktree directory is left on disk.
  1367â†’
  1368â†’Claude Code passes the path that WorktreeCreate printed on stdout as `worktree_path` in the hook input. This example reads that path and removes the directory:
  1369â†’
  1370â†’```json  theme={null}
  1371â†’{
  1372â†’  "hooks": {
  1373â†’    "WorktreeRemove": [
  1374â†’      {
  1375â†’        "hooks": [
  1376â†’          {
  1377â†’            "type": "command",
  1378â†’            "command": "bash -c 'jq -r .worktree_path | xargs rm -rf'"
  1379â†’          }
  1380â†’        ]
  1381â†’      }
  1382â†’    ]
  1383â†’  }
  1384â†’}
  1385â†’```
  1386â†’
  1387â†’#### WorktreeRemove input
  1388â†’
  1389â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), WorktreeRemove hooks receive the `worktree_path` field, which is the absolute path to the worktree being removed.
  1390â†’
  1391â†’```json  theme={null}
  1392â†’{
  1393â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1394â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1395â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1396â†’  "hook_event_name": "WorktreeRemove",
  1397â†’  "worktree_path": "/Users/.../my-project/.claude/worktrees/feature-auth"
  1398â†’}
  1399â†’```
  1400â†’
  1401â†’WorktreeRemove hooks have no decision control. They cannot block worktree removal but can perform cleanup tasks like removing version control state or archiving changes. Hook failures are logged in debug mode only. Only `type: "command"` hooks are supported.
  1402â†’
  1403â†’### PreCompact
  1404â†’
  1405â†’Runs before Claude Code is about to run a compact operation.
  1406â†’
  1407â†’The matcher value indicates whether compaction was triggered manually or automatically:
  1408â†’
  1409â†’| Matcher  | When it fires                                |
  1410â†’| :------- | :------------------------------------------- |
  1411â†’| `manual` | `/compact`                                   |
  1412â†’| `auto`   | Auto-compact when the context window is full |
  1413â†’
  1414â†’#### PreCompact input
  1415â†’
  1416â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), PreCompact hooks receive `trigger` and `custom_instructions`. For `manual`, `custom_instructions` contains what the user passes into `/compact`. For `auto`, `custom_instructions` is empty.
  1417â†’
  1418â†’```json  theme={null}
  1419â†’{
  1420â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1421â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1422â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1423â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1424â†’  "hook_event_name": "PreCompact",
  1425â†’  "trigger": "manual",
  1426â†’  "custom_instructions": ""
  1427â†’}
  1428â†’```
  1429â†’
  1430â†’### SessionEnd
  1431â†’
  1432â†’Runs when a Claude Code session ends. Useful for cleanup tasks, logging session
  1433â†’statistics, or saving session state. Supports matchers to filter by exit reason.
  1434â†’
  1435â†’The `reason` field in the hook input indicates why the session ended:
  1436â†’
  1437â†’| Reason                        | Description                                |
  1438â†’| :---------------------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
  1439â†’| `clear`                       | Session cleared with `/clear` command      |
  1440â†’| `logout`                      | User logged out                            |
  1441â†’| `prompt_input_exit`           | User exited while prompt input was visible |
  1442â†’| `bypass_permissions_disabled` | Bypass permissions mode was disabled       |
  1443â†’| `other`                       | Other exit reasons                         |
  1444â†’
  1445â†’#### SessionEnd input
  1446â†’
  1447â†’In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SessionEnd hooks receive a `reason` field indicating why the session ended. See the [reason table](#sessionend) above for all values.
  1448â†’
  1449â†’```json  theme={null}
  1450â†’{
  1451â†’  "session_id": "abc123",
  1452â†’  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1453â†’  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1454â†’  "permission_mode": "default",
  1455â†’  "hook_event_name": "SessionEnd",
  1456â†’  "reason": "other"
  1457â†’}
  1458â†’```
  1459â†’
  1460â†’SessionEnd hooks have no decision control. They cannot block session termination but can perform cleanup tasks.
  1461â†’
  1462â†’## Prompt-based hooks
  1463â†’
  1464â†’In addition to Bash command hooks (`type: "command"`), Claude Code supports prompt-based hooks (`type: "prompt"`) that use an LLM to evaluate whether to allow or block an action, and agent hooks (`type: "agent"`) that spawn an agentic verifier with tool access. Not all events support every hook type.
  1465â†’
  1466â†’Events that support all three hook types (`command`, `prompt`, and `agent`):
  1467â†’
  1468â†’* `PermissionRequest`
  1469â†’* `PostToolUse`
  1470â†’* `PostToolUseFailure`
  1471â†’* `PreToolUse`
  1472â†’* `Stop`
  1473â†’* `SubagentStop`
  1474â†’* `TaskCompleted`
  1475â†’* `UserPromptSubmit`
  1476â†’
  1477â†’Events that only support `type: "command"` hooks:
  1478â†’
  1479â†’* `ConfigChange`
  1480â†’* `Notification`
  1481â†’* `PreCompact`
  1482â†’* `SessionEnd`
  1483â†’* `SessionStart`
  1484â†’* `SubagentStart`
  1485â†’* `TeammateIdle`
  1486â†’* `WorktreeCreate`
  1487â†’* `WorktreeRemove`
  1488â†’
  1489â†’### How prompt-based hooks work
  1490â†’
  1491â†’Instead of executing a Bash command, prompt-based hooks:
  1492â†’
  1493â†’1. Send the hook input and your prompt to a Claude model, Haiku by default
  1494â†’2. The LLM responds with structured JSON containing a decision
  1495â†’3. Claude Code processes the decision automatically
  1496â†’
  1497â†’### Prompt hook configuration
  1498â†’
  1499â†’Set `type` to `"prompt"` and provide a `prompt` string instead of a `command`. Use the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder to inject the hook's JSON input data into your prompt text. Claude Code sends the combined prompt and input to a fast Claude model, which returns a JSON decision.
  1500â†’
  1501â†’This `Stop` hook asks the LLM to evaluate whether all tasks are complete before allowing Claude to finish:
  1502â†’
  1503â†’```json  theme={null}
  1504â†’{
  1505â†’  "hooks": {
  1506â†’    "Stop": [
  1507â†’      {
  1508â†’        "hooks": [
  1509â†’          {
  1510â†’            "type": "prompt",
  1511â†’            "prompt": "Evaluate if Claude should stop: $ARGUMENTS. Check if all tasks are complete."
  1512â†’          }
  1513â†’        ]
  1514â†’      }
  1515â†’    ]
  1516â†’  }
  1517â†’}
  1518â†’```
  1519â†’
  1520â†’| Field     | Required | Description                                                                                                                                                         |
  1521â†’| :-------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
  1522â†’| `type`    | yes      | Must be `"prompt"`                                                                                                                                                  |
  1523â†’| `prompt`  | yes      | The prompt text to send to the LLM. Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for the hook input JSON. If `$ARGUMENTS` is not present, input JSON is appended to the prompt |
  1524â†’| `model`   | no       | Model to use for evaluation. Defaults to a fast model                                                                                                               |
  1525â†’| `timeout` | no       | Timeout in seconds. Default: 30                                                                                                                                     |
  1526â†’
  1527â†’### Response schema
  1528â†’
  1529â†’The LLM must respond with JSON containing:
  1530â†’
  1531â†’```json  theme={null}
  1532â†’{
  1533â†’  "ok": true | false,
  1534â†’  "reason": "Explanation for the decision"
  1535â†’}
  1536â†’```
  1537â†’
  1538â†’| Field    | Description                                                |
  1539â†’| :------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- |
  1540â†’| `ok`     | `true` allows the action, `false` prevents it              |
  1541â†’| `reason` | Required when `ok` is `false`. Explanation shown to Claude |
  1542â†’
  1543â†’### Example: Multi-criteria Stop hook
  1544â†’
  1545â†’This `Stop` hook uses a detailed prompt to check three conditions before allowing Claude to stop. If `"ok"` is `false`, Claude continues working with the provided reason as its next instruction. `SubagentStop` hooks use the same format to evaluate whether a [subagent](/en/sub-agents) should stop:
  1546â†’
  1547â†’```json  theme={null}
  1548â†’{
  1549â†’  "hooks": {
  1550â†’    "Stop": [
  1551â†’      {
  1552â†’        "hooks": [
  1553â†’          {
  1554â†’            "type": "prompt",
  1555â†’            "prompt": "You are evaluating whether Claude should stop working. Context: $ARGUMENTS\n\nAnalyze the conversation and determine if:\n1. All user-requested tasks are complete\n2. Any errors need to be addressed\n3. Follow-up work is needed\n\nRespond with JSON: {\"ok\": true} to allow stopping, or {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"your explanation\"} to continue working.",
  1556â†’            "timeout": 30
  1557â†’          }
  1558â†’        ]
  1559â†’      }
  1560â†’    ]
  1561â†’  }
  1562â†’}
  1563â†’```
  1564â†’
  1565â†’## Agent-based hooks
  1566â†’
  1567â†’Agent-based hooks (`type: "agent"`) are like prompt-based hooks but with multi-turn tool access. Instead of a single LLM call, an agent hook spawns a subagent that can read files, search code, and inspect the codebase to verify conditions. Agent hooks support the same events as prompt-based hooks.
  1568â†’
  1569â†’### How agent hooks work
  1570â†’
  1571â†’When an agent hook fires:
  1572â†’
  1573â†’1. Claude Code spawns a subagent with your prompt and the hook's JSON input
  1574â†’2. The subagent can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to investigate
  1575â†’3. After up to 50 turns, the subagent returns a structured `{ "ok": true/false }` decision
  1576â†’4. Claude Code processes the decision the same way as a prompt hook
  1577â†’
  1578â†’Agent hooks are useful when verification requires inspecting actual files or test output, not just evaluating the hook input data alone.
  1579â†’
  1580â†’### Agent hook configuration
  1581â†’
  1582â†’Set `type` to `"agent"` and provide a `prompt` string. The configuration fields are the same as [prompt hooks](#prompt-hook-configuration), with a longer default timeout:
  1583â†’
  1584â†’| Field     | Required | Description                                                                                 |
  1585â†’| :-------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
  1586â†’| `type`    | yes      | Must be `"agent"`                                                                           |
  1587â†’| `prompt`  | yes      | Prompt describing what to verify. Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for the hook input JSON |
  1588â†’| `model`   | no       | Model to use. Defaults to a fast model                                                      |
  1589â†’| `timeout` | no       | Timeout in seconds. Default: 60                                                             |
  1590â†’
  1591â†’The response schema is the same as prompt hooks: `{ "ok": true }` to allow or `{ "ok": false, "reason": "..." }` to block.
  1592â†’
  1593â†’This `Stop` hook verifies that all unit tests pass before allowing Claude to finish:
  1594â†’
  1595â†’```json  theme={null}
  1596â†’{
  1597â†’  "hooks": {
  1598â†’    "Stop": [
  1599â†’      {
  1600â†’        "hooks": [
  1601â†’          {
  1602â†’            "type": "agent",
  1603â†’            "prompt": "Verify that all unit tests pass. Run the test suite and check the results. $ARGUMENTS",
  1604â†’            "timeout": 120
  1605â†’          }
  1606â†’        ]
  1607â†’      }
  1608â†’    ]
  1609â†’  }
  1610â†’}
  1611â†’```
  1612â†’
  1613â†’## Run hooks in the background
  1614â†’
  1615â†’By default, hooks block Claude's execution until they complete. For long-running tasks like deployments, test suites, or external API calls, set `"async": true` to run the hook in the background while Claude continues working. Async hooks cannot block or control Claude's behavior: response fields like `decision`, `permissionDecision`, and `continue` have no effect, because the action they would have controlled has already completed.
  1616â†’
  1617â†’### Configure an async hook
  1618â†’
  1619â†’Add `"async": true` to a command hook's configuration to run it in the background without blocking Claude. This field is only available on `type: "command"` hooks.
  1620â†’
  1621â†’This hook runs a test script after every `Write` tool call. Claude continues working immediately while `run-tests.sh` executes for up to 120 seconds. When the script finishes, its output is delivered on the next conversation turn:
  1622â†’
  1623â†’```json  theme={null}
  1624â†’{
  1625â†’  "hooks": {
  1626â†’    "PostToolUse": [
  1627â†’      {
  1628â†’        "matcher": "Write",
  1629â†’        "hooks": [
  1630â†’          {
  1631â†’            "type": "command",
  1632â†’            "command": "/path/to/run-tests.sh",
  1633â†’            "async": true,
  1634â†’            "timeout": 120
  1635â†’          }
  1636â†’        ]
  1637â†’      }
  1638â†’    ]
  1639â†’  }
  1640â†’}
  1641â†’```
  1642â†’
  1643â†’The `timeout` field sets the maximum time in seconds for the background process. If not specified, async hooks use the same 10-minute default as sync hooks.
  1644â†’
  1645â†’### How async hooks execute
  1646â†’
  1647â†’When an async hook fires, Claude Code starts the hook process and immediately continues without waiting for it to finish. The hook receives the same JSON input via stdin as a synchronous hook.
  1648â†’
  1649â†’After the background process exits, if the hook produced a JSON response with a `systemMessage` or `additionalContext` field, that content is delivered to Claude as context on the next conversation turn.
  1650â†’
  1651â†’### Example: run tests after file changes
  1652â†’
  1653â†’This hook starts a test suite in the background whenever Claude writes a file, then reports the results back to Claude when the tests finish. Save this script to `.claude/hooks/run-tests-async.sh` in your project and make it executable with `chmod +x`:
  1654â†’
  1655â†’```bash  theme={null}
  1656â†’#!/bin/bash
  1657â†’# run-tests-async.sh
  1658â†’
  1659â†’# Read hook input from stdin
  1660â†’INPUT=$(cat)
  1661â†’FILE_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')
  1662â†’
  1663â†’# Only run tests for source files
  1664â†’if [[ "$FILE_PATH" != *.ts && "$FILE_PATH" != *.js ]]; then
  1665â†’  exit 0
  1666â†’fi
  1667â†’
  1668â†’# Run tests and report results via systemMessage
  1669â†’RESULT=$(npm test 2>&1)
  1670â†’EXIT_CODE=$?
  1671â†’
  1672â†’if [ $EXIT_CODE -eq 0 ]; then
  1673â†’  echo "{\"systemMessage\": \"Tests passed after editing $FILE_PATH\"}"
  1674â†’else
  1675â†’  echo "{\"systemMessage\": \"Tests failed after editing $FILE_PATH: $RESULT\"}"
  1676â†’fi
  1677â†’```
  1678â†’
  1679â†’Then add this configuration to `.claude/settings.json` in your project root. The `async: true` flag lets Claude keep working while tests run:
  1680â†’
  1681â†’```json  theme={null}
  1682â†’{
  1683â†’  "hooks": {
  1684â†’    "PostToolUse": [
  1685â†’      {
  1686â†’        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
  1687â†’        "hooks": [
  1688â†’          {
  1689â†’            "type": "command",
  1690â†’            "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/run-tests-async.sh",
  1691â†’            "async": true,
  1692â†’            "timeout": 300
  1693â†’          }
  1694â†’        ]
  1695â†’      }
  1696â†’    ]
  1697â†’  }
  1698â†’}
  1699â†’```
  1700â†’
  1701â†’### Limitations
  1702â†’
  1703â†’Async hooks have several constraints compared to synchronous hooks:
  1704â†’
  1705â†’* Only `type: "command"` hooks support `async`. Prompt-based hooks cannot run asynchronously.
  1706â†’* Async hooks cannot block tool calls or return decisions. By the time the hook completes, the triggering action has already proceeded.
  1707â†’* Hook output is delivered on the next conversation turn. If the session is idle, the response waits until the next user interaction.
  1708â†’* Each execution creates a separate background process. There is no deduplication across multiple firings of the same async hook.
  1709â†’
  1710â†’## Security considerations
  1711â†’
  1712â†’### Disclaimer
  1713â†’
  1714â†’Hooks run with your system user's full permissions.
  1715â†’
  1716â†’<Warning>
  1717â†’  Hooks execute shell commands with your full user permissions. They can modify, delete, or access any files your user account can access. Review and test all hook commands before adding them to your configuration.
  1718â†’</Warning>
  1719â†’
  1720â†’### Security best practices
  1721â†’
  1722â†’Keep these practices in mind when writing hooks:
  1723â†’
  1724â†’* **Validate and sanitize inputs**: never trust input data blindly
  1725â†’* **Always quote shell variables**: use `"$VAR"` not `$VAR`
  1726â†’* **Block path traversal**: check for `..` in file paths
  1727â†’* **Use absolute paths**: specify full paths for scripts, using `"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"` for the project root
  1728â†’* **Skip sensitive files**: avoid `.env`, `.git/`, keys, etc.
  1729â†’
  1730â†’## Debug hooks
  1731â†’
  1732â†’Run `claude --debug` to see hook execution details, including which hooks matched, their exit codes, and output. Toggle verbose mode with `Ctrl+O` to see hook progress in the transcript.
  1733â†’
  1734â†’```
  1735â†’[DEBUG] Executing hooks for PostToolUse:Write
  1736â†’[DEBUG] Getting matching hook commands for PostToolUse with query: Write
  1737â†’[DEBUG] Found 1 hook matchers in settings
  1738â†’[DEBUG] Matched 1 hooks for query "Write"
  1739â†’[DEBUG] Found 1 hook commands to execute
  1740â†’[DEBUG] Executing hook command: <Your command> with timeout 600000ms
  1741â†’[DEBUG] Hook command completed with status 0: <Your stdout>
  1742â†’```
  1743â†’
  1744â†’For troubleshooting common issues like hooks not firing, infinite Stop hook loops, or configuration errors, see [Limitations and troubleshooting](/en/hooks-guide#limitations-and-troubleshooting) in the guide.
  1745â†’
